coredata.dc:title: Carbon dot / thermo-responsive polymer capacitive wavelength-specific photodetector
coredata.dc:creator: Shauloff, Nitzan
Prishkolnik, Noa
Singh, Seema
Manikandan, Rajendran
Ben Nun, Uri
Jelinek, Raz
coredata.dc:description: Organic photodetectors are considered attractive alternatives to inorganic, semiconductor-based devices. We constructed a wavelength-specific capacitive photodetector, comprising carbon dots (C-dots) and poly-(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM), a thermo-responsive polymer. Upon illuminating the C-dot/PNIPAM composite, light absorbed by the C-dots is released as heat; the photothermal effect gives rise to a rapid temperature increase con-sequently affecting structural transformation of the PNIPAM host and concomitant change in the recorded capacitance. Importantly, wavelength sensitivity of the photodetector is determined by the excitation wavelength of the embedded C-dots; we show that tuning the wavelength response of the system can be readily achieved through selection of the C-dot species incorporated within the PNIPAM matrix. The C-dot/PNIPAM photodetector ex-hibits reversibility and rapid light response, is synthesized from inexpensive and recyclable building blocks. The technology may be employed for photo-switching, photoinduced ca-pacitors, smart windows, and other photo-electronic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
PNIPAM
Smart hydrogel
Photodetector hydrogel
Capacitive photodetector

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical and optical properties of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We acknowledge financial support by the Israel Science Foundation , grant number 20/3553 .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation and physicochemical properties of the carbon-dot/polyNIPAM composite. A. Molecular organization and light-induced transformation of the C-dot/PNIPAM composite hydrogel. Light exposure induces the hydrophilic-to-hydrophobic phase transformation of PNIPAM, resulting in water release and optical transformation – from dark and transparent appearance into whitish-opaque. B. Temperature-dependent transmittance of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel; photographs in the inset depict the visual change of the composite material. Left: 25 °C. Right: after heating to 40 °C. C. DSC thermogram underscoring the LCST recorded for the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel. D. SEM images of bare PNIPAM (i) and C-dot/PNIPAM (ii).

Photothermal effect induced in the carbon dot/PNIPAM composite. A. Thermal (infrared) images (top row) and regular photographs (bottom row) of bare PNIPAM (left) and C-dot/PNIPAM (right) upon illumination by a solar simulator (power of 70 mW/cm2) at the indicated durations. Colour code corresponding to the thermal images is shown on the right. B. Temperature increase recorded in the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel at different C-dot concentrations.

Capacitive light sensing properties of the carbon dot/PNIPAM photodetector. A. scheme of the capacitive photodetector prepared via embedding the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel in a plastic cuvette plated on two sides with thin copper sheets. B. Temporal analysis of the light-induced capacitive signal recorded by the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel photodetector upon illumination of 60 s (datapoints collected every 0.03 s). C. Photoinduced capacitive change cycles – illumination was for 60 s (generated by a solar simulator at 70 mW/cm2, datapoints collected every 0.3 s). D. Capacitance increase recorded upon different irradiation times. E. Relationship between C-dot concentration in the C-dot/PNIPAM matrix and capacitance increase recorded upon illumination of the device for 60 s (generated by a solar simulator at 70 mW/cm2).

Wavelength selectivity of the carbon dot/PNIPAM photodetector accomplished through embedding different C-dots. A. Preparation of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel using C-dots exhibiting different colours under UV light illumination (356 nm). B. Normalized fluorescence excitation spectra of the three C-dots produced. C. Capacitance changes recorded in C-dot/PNIPAM photodetectors comprising the different C-dots (blue, yellow, red) upon illumination at different wavelengths. D. Principal components analysis (PCA) plot showing capacitive response cluster differentiation according to wavelength illumination.

head.ce:title: Carbon dot / thermo-responsive polymer capacitive wavelength-specific photodetector

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, 84105, Beer Sheva, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, 84105, Beer Sheva, Israel

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Ilse Katz Institute for Nanotechnology, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, 84105, Beer Sheva, Israel

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Ilse Katz Institute for Nanotechnology, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, 84105, Beer Sheva, Israel

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Chemistry, Ben Gurion University of the Negev, 84105, Beer Sheva, Israel.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Ben Gurion University of the Negev

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic photodetectors are considered attractive alternatives to inorganic, semiconductor-based devices. We constructed a wavelength-specific capacitive photodetector, comprising carbon dots (C-dots) and poly-(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM), a thermo-responsive polymer. Upon illuminating the C-dot/PNIPAM composite, light absorbed by the C-dots is released as heat; the photothermal effect gives rise to a rapid temperature increase con-sequently affecting structural transformation of the PNIPAM host and concomitant change in the recorded capacitance. Importantly, wavelength sensitivity of the photodetector is determined by the excitation wavelength of the embedded C-dots; we show that tuning the wavelength response of the system can be readily achieved through selection of the C-dot species incorporated within the PNIPAM matrix. The C-dot/PNIPAM photodetector ex-hibits reversibility and rapid light response, is synthesized from inexpensive and recyclable building blocks. The technology may be employed for photo-switching, photoinduced ca-pacitors, smart windows, and other photo-electronic applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Stimuli-responsive “smart materials”, which can alter their structures and properties in response to external effects, have attracted significant interest [1,2]. Such external stimuli have included temperature [3], voltage [4], mechanical stress [5], light-irradiation [6], pH change [7], alterations in ionic strength [8], and others. Temperature-responsive polymers (also referred to as temperature responsive hydrogels) constitute one of the largest classes of stimuli responsive polymers [9–11]. In many instances, such materials display a threshold temperature – the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) – below which the hydrogel is hydrophilic, whereas above the LCST a phase separation occurs and water molecules are ejected from the material [12]. Polya(N-isopropylacrylamide) (PNIPAM) has been a widely studied thermo-responsive hydrogel exhibiting LCST of ∼32 °C in aqueous media [13,14]. Specifically, PNIPAM undergoes a reversible hydrophilic-to-hydrophobic phase transition at the LCST, accompanied by a significant change in transparency [15], and electrostatic permittivity [16].

Light has also been employed as an inducer of transformations in stimuli-responsive hydrogels [17]. Varied substances have been embedded in such materials to achieve photo-response, including photochromic molecules [18], photocleavable residues [19], and photoactive nanoparticles [20]. A recent study has employed polypyrrole nanotube (PPyNT)-doped PNIPAM hydrogel as a light and temperature-switchable smart material for a supercapacitor electrode [6]. Another example has been the addition of surface-modified Fe3O4-nanoparticles to PNIPAM hydrogel for creating a visible–light–triggered hydrogel actuators [21]. Such systems have limitations as possible versatile photodetectors, primarily restricted wavelength range, insufficient tunability, and complex synthesis and fabrication processes.

Carbon dots (C-dots), a class of multifunctional carbonaceous nanoparticles, have attracted significant interest due to their unique physicochemical properties [22,23]. C-dots are readily synthesized from varied building blocks, and exhibit tunable fluorescence [24]. As such, C-dots are particularly promising as conduits for optoelectronic applications due to their broad range of fluorescence emission [25], photothermal effects [26], low photobleaching and high stability [27,28]. Importantly, C-dots can be easily integrated within varied composite materials, and have been employed as light-sensitive components in photodetector designs [29–31].

Recently, we demonstrated that C-dots can be encapsulated within PNIPAM hydrogel and mediate its phase transformation upon light illumination [32]. Here, we fabricated a capacitance-based C-dot/PNIPAM photodetector. We show that the C-dot/PNIPAM composite hydrogel functions as an effective dielectric medium in the capacitive device. Upon light illumination, the embedded C-dots release heat to their environs, increasing the temperature above the LCST of PNIPAM, thereby altering the dielectric properties of the matrix and giving rise to a significant capacitance change. Notably, wavelength specificity of the photodetector was accomplished through embedding in the PNIPAM hydrogel C-dots having different excitation wavelengths. Overall, the capacitive C-dot/PNIPAM photodetector furnishes an easily prepared and tunable light sensitive platform, which may be also employed in light-induced energy storage.

N-Isopropylacrylamide (NIPAM), Citric acid, urea, o-phenylenediamine (oPD), p-phenylenediamine (pPA), Potassium persulfate (KPS), N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA), N,N,N′,N'-tetramethyl-ethylenediamine (TEMED) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. Ethanol (EtOH) and Deuterium oxide (D2O) were purchased from Bio-Lab Ltd, Jerusalem, Israel. (DMSO) was obtained from Glentham Life Sciences. Copper Conductive tape 6.3 mm W X 16.46m L, TED PELLA,INC. uv cuvette, Polystyrene size 10 m (SARSTEDT International). Interdigitated gold electrodes (Dimensions: 10 x 6 × 0.75 mm; glass substrate; Insulating layer: EPON SU8 resin; electrode material: Au; electrode thickness: 150 nm; microelectrode with: 10 μm, microelectrode gap: 10 μm; number of fingers: 90 pairs; surface of tested area: 0.86 mm2) were purchased from MicruX Technologies (Oviedo, Spain).

Synthesis of the C-dots was based upon reported procedures using a hydrothermal treatment strategy [33]. Briefly, citric acid (1 g) and urea (3 g) were dissolved in 15 mL of DMSO and the transparent solution was then transferred to poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (Teflon)-lined autoclave. The solution was heated at 160 °C for 4 h in the autoclave followed by cooling to room temperature. The C-dot suspension obtained was mixed with excess ethanol and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove polymeric by-products. Dialysis was then performed in water for 1 days using dialysis tubing (Avg. flat width 9 mm (0.35 in.), MWCO 2000) cleaning the solution from unreacted precursors. An intense black powder was obtained after evaporation.

The blue, yellow, and red C-dots were also synthesized by hydrothermal treatment according to a previously reported method with small modification [34,35]. Specifically, for the blue C-dots preparation we used 1 g of citric acid dissolved in 5 ml of water, treated hydrothermally at 180 °C for 12 h. Yellow C-dots were synthesized using 1 g of ortho-phenylenediamine dissolved in 5 ml ethanol/water mixture (80% ethanol 20% water), followed by 15 min sonication. After dissolving the solution was at 180 °C for 12 h. Red C-dots were prepared from 1 g of para-phenylenediamine dissolved in 5 ml ethanol, followed by 15 min sonication, and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. Dialysis was then performed to all samples in water for 1 day using dialysis tubing (Avg. flat width 9 mm (0.35 in.), MWCO 2000). C-dots powder was obtained after solvent evaporation.

For the preparation of C-dot/PNIPAM composite hydrogel, 450 mg of the monomer NIPAM and 15 mg of the cross-linker N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAM) were dissolved in 5 mL water and sonicated for 2 min 15 mg of C-dots were added to the solution and stirred for 4 h at room temperature until a homogeneous solution was obtained. The hydrogel was then formed through free radical polymerization by adding initiator potassium persulfate (KPS) (5 mg in 0.2 mL of water), and stirred for 10 min. The solution was then bubbled with an inert gas (Ar), and subsequently 15 μL of the catalyst N, N, N′,N′-tetramethyl-ethylenediamine (TEMED) was added. The solution was then poured into 1 ml cuvettes and kept at room temperature for 1h (until hydrogel formation was viewed). For complete polymerization, the cuvettes were kept at 4 °C for 12 h sealed with Parafilm. The C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel capacitive photodetector devices were prepared by placing thin copper sheets in the inner sides of the cuvettes acting as the conductive electrodes.

Capacitive photo response measurements of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel devices were carried out using an LCR meter (Keysight Technologies, E4980AL Precision LCR Meter). For the light source, a white light emitting diode (LED, Chanzon, China) operating in a wavelength range of 380–800 nm and mounted LED sources with wavelength centred at 340-nm (UV), 365-nm (UV), 430-nm, 470-nm, 490-nm, 563-nm LEDs from Thorlabs (Newton, New Jersey, United States) were used. Light intensity was measured using a power meter (PM100, Thorlabs) while applying the illumination. To deliver similar power to a specific area the approach we used a focusing lens to control the beam size and shape, which can help to ensure selection of the irradiation area. The power level was adjusted by varying the input power using attenuators to reduce the power as needed. We set every light resource to 70 mW/cm2. Capacitive measurements were performed at room temperature after exposing the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel devices to the different light sources. Capacitance values were recorded after reaching plateaus, collecting the data every 0.03 and 0.3 s at 1 kHz constant frequency at a voltage of 1 V. Thorlabs DC2200a LED driver was used in the pulsed light experiments.

The photoinduced capacitance depends on variations of the dielectric constant of the hydrogel matrix. The capacitance of the photodetector device was defined by the Equation:
(1)
C =
ε 0
ε r
l t
d
where C is the capacitance in farads (F), ε0 is the permittivity of free space (ε0 = 8.854 × 10−12 F/m), εr is the relative permittivity, also known as the dielectric constant, t is the thickness of the copper electrode, l is the length of copper electrode and d is the distance between the electrodes. The capacitance response of the sensors – ΔC - was defined as Clight – Cdark, where Clight and Cdark are the capacitance value after illumination and the capacitance baseline value measured under dark conditions, respectively. Capacitance change was calculated to compare between the electrodes.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of the hydrogels were acquired on dried sample coated with 5 nm of Au sputtering. The samples were imaged using a FEI Verios, SEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, XHR 460L). The images were viewed at different magnifications, in an acceleration voltage of 5 kV.
UV–vis absorbance spectra were recorded on Thermo Scientific Evolution 220 spectrophotometer in the range of 300–800 nm at room temperature.

Fluorescence spectra of C-dot solutions were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba, Japan). Fluorescence emissions were measured at different excitation wavelengths ranging from 360 nm to 620 nm. The fluorescence was measured at a 90° angle relative to the excitation light. This geometry is used instead of placing the sensor at the line of the excitation light at a 180° angle to avoid interference of the transmitted excitation light. Fluorescence spectra of the deposited films on a silicon wafer were acquired on a Newport Corp. MS257 spectrometer equipped with a Si CCD detector and long-pass order-sorting filters. A 1-mW He–Cd laser was used for excitation.

Differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) was performed on a DSC823e calorimeter (Mettler Toledo, Switzerland). The gel samples were equilibrated with water, and analysis was carried out by heating the samples from 10 to 60 °C, at a linear heating rate of 3 °C/min, with water as a reference.
Thermal imaging of the gels to determine the temperature before and after illumination with light was carried out by a thermographic camera (FLIR i7). The temperature range was locked to 23–41 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical and optical properties of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 depicts the synthesis scheme and physicochemical characterization of the C-dot/PNIPAM composite. The preparation process of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel is illustrated in Fig. 1A. C-dots exhibiting different colours (i.e. different excitation/emission profiles) were synthesized from phenyldiamine, citric acid and urea as the carbonaceous precursors. The C-dots were mixed with the NIPAM monomer in deionized water and polymerization was performed in a confined space of the sealed cuvette, followed by free-radical polymerization of NIPAM to produce the cross-linked PNIPAM hydrogel. Importantly, the formation of a physical C-dot/PNIPAM mixture (rather than covalent attachment of the C-dots to PNIPAM) allowed retaining of the unique thermos-responsive properties of PNIPAM, specifically the reversible hydrophilic-to-hydrophobic phase transition at the lower critical solution temperature (LCST) [36]. Fig. 1A particularly highlights the key role of the C-dots in inducing the phase transition of PNIPAM. The embedded C-dots adsorb incident light which subsequently dissipates as heat, giving rise to the phase transition of the PNIPAM matrix.

Fig. 1A underscores the pronounced change in visual appearance of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel associated with the thermal phase transition, from semitransparent dark appearance to a whitish opaque hydrogel. This transformation corresponds to the predominance of hydrogen bonding between water molecules and amide residues in the PNIPAM sidechains at lower temperatures (below the LCST). As a result of these interactions, the PNIPAM sidechains are effectively solubilized in water giving rise to optical transparency. In contrast, when the temperature is higher than the LCST, the hydrogen bonds between the amide residues and water molecules are weakened, inducing association of the hydrophobic units within the sidechains and consequent aggregation which go hand in hand with the optical transformation [37].

The thermal-induced optical transformation of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel is manifested in Fig. 1B, depicting the transmittance (at 800 nm; the full spectrum is shown in Fig. S1). Specifically, at 25 °C, the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel exhibited transmisttance of around 33%, reduced to almost 0% above 32 °C, reflecting the opaque nature of the C-dot/PNIPAM matrix; representative photographs of the cuvette at 24 °C (left) and 40 °C (right), shown in Fig. 1B, demonstrate the pronounced visual transformation of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel. The differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) thermogram recorded for C-dot/PNIPAM (Fig. 1C) reveals a dip at ∼32 °C, accounting for the LCST of PNIPAM [38]. (the DSC thermogram of PNIPAM alone without embedded C-dots in Fig. S2 features the same transition temperature as the C-dot/PNIPAM). Together, the optical and thermodynamic results in Fig. 1B and C inidcate that the presence of the C-dots did not disrupt the thermal, cooperative properties of PNIPAM.

The scanning electron microscopy (SEM) analysis in Fig. 1D illuminates the internal morphology of the hydrogels, both in the case of PNIPAM alone (Fig. 1D,i) and the C-dot/PNIPAM composite (Fig. 1D,ii). Interestingly, the SEM image of the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel indicates that while the overall microporous morphology of PNIPAM was retained upon incorporation of the C-dots, a network of smaller pores was also apparent (Fig. 1D,ii). This microporous organization is functionally important as it may aid efficient photoinduced dissipation of heat generated by the embedded C-dots. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectral analysis in Fig. S3 further confirms incorporation of the C-dots within the PNIPAM host matrix.

Fig. 2 depicts the photothermal properties of the C-dot/PNIPAM composite. The thermal images in Fig. 2A were recorded upon placing the hydrogel in a transparent 1 ml cuvette and illuminating for different time durations with a solar simulator at a power of 70 mW/cm2. Notably, the thermal images in Fig. 2A reveal that the temperature of the C-dot/PNIPAM increased from 23 °C to more than 37 °C within 180-sec irradiation, while a cuvette containing bare PNIPAM (without embedded C-dots, appearing colourless) underwent markedly lower temperature increase. The pronounced photo-induced heating apparent in the case of C-dot/PNIPAM attests to the key role of the C-dots in facilitating the photothermal effect.

The graph in Fig. 2B showing the relationship between the photoinduced temperature increase and concentration of C-dots in the PNIPAM host matrix further accounts for the essential role of the carbon nanoparticles. Indeed, Fig. 2B demonstrates that higher C-dot concentrations affected greater temperature increase of C-dot/PNIPAM at all illumination times. Interestingly, the maximal temperature increase was observed at a concentration of 15 mg/ml, while a higher C-dot concentration (20 mg/mL) gave rise to a lower temperature increase. This result is likely due to the aggregation of C-dots at a concentration of 20 mg/mL, giving rise to lesser photoinduced heat dissipation.

Exploiting the dielectric properties of C-dots [39] and PNIPAM [40], we employed the C-dot/PNIPAM composite as a sensitive, wavelength-selective, and fast-responsive photodetector (Figs. 3–4
). Fig. 3 presents the capacitive photodetection properties of C-dot/PNIPAM. Fig. 3A illustrates the experimental setup. The C-dot/NIPAM mixture was polymerized in situ within a transparent cuvette coated on two sides by copper sheets, thereby creating a capacitor. The photodetection experiments were carried out at room temperature (25 °C) through illuminating the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel-containing cuvette and recording the capacitance changes (at a potential of 1 V and frequency of 1 kHz).

Fig. 3B presents a representative photoinduced capacitance signal obtained using the device schematically shown in Fig. 3A. In the experiment, we illuminated the C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel for 60 s using a solar simulator (power of 70 mW/cm2), and continuously recorded the capacitance. Notably, a capacitance increase was concurrent with illumination, dropping back to the initial, baseline value upon turning off the light. The capacitance returned to its initial value approximately 4 min after ceasing illumination, indicating that the photodetector could be effectively regenerated. The photo-cycling experiment in Fig. 3C further underscores the stability and recyclability of the C-dot/PNIPAM device. Specifically, the photoinduced capacitive signals (1-min illumination) appear uniform even upon application of several on/off cycles.

The dependence of photoinduced capacitive change upon light pulse duration is shown in Fig. 3D. The almost linear relationship between the capacitance-increase and illumination duration up to approximately 90 s confirms the dependence of the capacitance change upon hydrogel temperature. Additionally, a relationship between light intensity and photoinduced capacitive signals was observed upon the use of varying power levels of the illuminating light beam (Fig. S4). The dependence of the capacitive photo-response upon C-dot concentration is portrayed in Fig. 3E. Consistent with the photothermal analysis in Fig. 2B, an embedded C-dot concentration of 15 mg/ml yielded the most pronounced capacitance change. Importantly, the increase in capacitance of over 10 nF is significant, attesting to the high sensitivity of the C-dot/PNIPAM-based photodetector.

As the experiments in Figs. 2 and 3 established a clear link between the photoinduced capacitive sensing and light absorbance by the encapsulation of C-dots, we further examined wavelength selectivity of the C-dot/PNIPAM photodetector using C-dots exhibiting different absorbance profiles (Fig. 4). Fig. 4A illustrates preparation of the C-dot/PNIPAM composite using NIPAM monomeric units, N,N′-methylenebisacrylamide (MBAA) as the cross-linker) and C-dots exhibiting different fluorescence colours (i.e., distinct excitation/emission properties) prepared from phenylenediamine as the carbonaceous precursor. C-dots exhibiting distinct excitation/emission profiles can be produced from phenylenediamine through modulating the synthesis conditions [41]. Photographs of the resultant C-dot/PNIPAM composite gels upon illumination at 365 nm are presented in Fig. 4A.

The normalized fluorescence excitation spectra in Fig. 4B show the specific wavelengths absorbed by the synthesized C-dots employed - 350 nm, 416 nm, and 490 nm for blue C-dots, yellow C-dots, and red C-dots, respectively. Full fluorescence mapping of each C-dot is presented in Fig. S5; the UV–vis absorbance spectra of the C-dots (Fig. S6) underscore the distinct wavelengths absorbed by the C-dots. Atomic force microscopy (AFM, Fig. S7). Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra (Fig. S8), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Table S1) furnish comprehensive characterization of the C-dots. Fig. 4C depicts the wavelength dependence of the capacitive response using light emitting diodes (LEDs) in the range of 340–563 nm. Notably, the graph in Fig. 4C reveals that the maximal capacitive response of each C-dot/PNIPAM photodetector is dependent upon the embedded C-dot species. Indeed, the most pronounced capacitive change occurred upon “matching”, or overlap, between the wavelength of illuminating light and the excitation maximum of the embedded C-dots. For example, illumination with a 430 nm LED gave rise to a maximal capacitance response – of around 4.5 nF - in the case of the yellow-C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel photodetector (Fig. 4C, yellow signals). This photodetector, however, produced lower capacitive signals (less than 2 nF) when illuminated by the LEDs emitting at 340 nm or 563 nm. The difference in capacitance response is related to the maximal fluorescence excitations. Thus, C-dots with higher fluorescence excitation at a specific wavelength will generate greater amount of light-to-heat conversion, leading to a phase transition that will increase the capacitance values of the photodetector. Similarly, the most pronounced capacitance change upon illumination at 340 nm was recorded for the blue-C-dots/PNIPAM hydrogel photodetector, while the red-C-dots/PNIPAM hydrogel photodetector generated the highest capacitive signal upon illumination at 563 nm.

The capacitive response data obtained upon illumination of the three C-dots/PNIPAM hydrogels (each contain PNIPAM hydrogel with embedded C-dot comprising a different C-dot species) with various monochromatic illumination were classified according to principal component analysis (PCA) (Fig. 4D). The PCA graph in Fig. 4D underscores the capability of the C-dots/PNIPAM hydrogel photodetector to distinguish among wavelengths of illuminating light. Specifically, Fig. 4D depicts the score plot in the first two principal component space in which PCA1 accounts for the greatest total variation at 60.63% and PCA2 for the variation of 33.37% (with the total of 94%). Each point represents three independent capacitive measurements. Importantly, clustering of the experimental datapoints in the PCA plot shows almost no overlaps between the wavelengths tested. Indeed, even though C-dots exhibit in general broad excitation spectra (e.g., Fig. 4B) The use of an electrode array (utilizing three different C-dots/PNIPAM hydrogels) allows distinguishing among wavelengths. The photo-induced capacitive “fingerprints” depicted in Fig. 4C, obtained with just three hydrogels, underscore the power of the photodetector to distinguish wavelengths of incident light; increasing the number of the photodetector array elements (i.e., preparing hydrogel electrodes with C-dots having different light excitation profiles) would further enhance the wavelength discrimination capabilities.

We constructed a capacitive wavelength-specific organic photodetector comprising C-dots and PNIPAM, a thermo-responsive polymer. The simple device displays rapid, reversible capacitance transformations upon light illumination. The capacitive photo-response corresponds to the photothermal effects associated with the C-dots embedded in the PNIPAM matrix, giving rise to phase transition of the polymer close to the LCST and concomitant change in the dielectric constant. Notably, wavelength selectivity of the photodetector directly depends upon the excitation profile of the embedded C-dots; therefore, tuning the wavelength response can be accomplished through selection of the embedded C-dots. While we present here wavelength specificity in the range of 340–560 nm, the wavelength response can be further extended by incorporating C-dots with suitable optical properties. Overall, the biodegradable and recyclable C-dot/PNIPAM hydrogel, synthesized from inexpensive and readily available carbonaceous precursors, can be employed as a useful conduit for photo switches, hydrogel photo-capacitors, smart windows, and other photoelectronic applications.

Nitzan Shauloff: conducted the laboratory work, analysis and interpretation of the results, Formal analysis, came up with the, Conceptualization, read and edited the manuscript, Writing – review & editing. Noa Prishkolnik: conducted the laboratory work, analysis and interpretation of the results, Formal analysis, read and edited the manuscript, Writing – review & editing. Seema Singh: came up with the, Conceptualization. Rajendran Manikandan: helped with the, Investigation. Uri Ben Nun: helped with the, Investigation, This work was completed through the contributions of all authors. Raz Jelinek: came up with the, Conceptualization, read and edited the manuscript, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support by the Israel Science Foundation, grant number 20/3553.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.carbon.2023.118211.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Responsive hydrogels – structurally and dimensionally optimized smart frameworks for applications in catalysis, micro-system technology and material science

Kuckling, Responsive hydrogels - structurally and dimensionally optimized smart frameworks for applications in catalysis, micro-system technology and material science, Chem. Soc. Rev. 42, 7391-7420 (2013). https://doi.org/10.1039/c3cs60031a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging applications of stimuli-responsive polymer materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.A.C. Stuart, W.T.S. Huck, J. Genzer, M. Muller, C. Ober, M. Stamm, G.B. Sukhorukov, I. Szleifer, V. V. Tsukruk, M. Urban, F. Winnik, S. Zauscher, I. Luzinov, S. Minko, Emerging applications of stimuli-responsive polymer materials, Nat. Mater. 9, 101-113 (2010). https://doi.org/10.1038/nmat2614.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Temperature- and light-responsive smart polymer materials

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Voltage-responsive vesicles based on orthogonal assembly of two homopolymers
